stem:
A bakery introduced a loyalty card and then saw repeat visits rise. The owner concludes that the card caused the increase in repeat visits.

The owner's conclusion depends on which of the following assumptions?

choices.text: No other change that could raise repeat visits occurred during the same period.
Customers prefer loyalty cards to discount coupons.
Repeat visits are more profitable than first-time visits.
Every customer signed up for the loyalty card.
The bakery's prices matched those of competitors.

distractorRationale.B: Whether customers like cards better than coupons is irrelevant; the owner only needs to rule out other causes of the rise, not compare promotion types.
distractorRationale.C: The profitability of repeat visits says nothing about what caused them to increase, which is the gap the assumption must fill.
distractorRationale.D: The conclusion does not require universal sign-up; even partial adoption could explain a rise, so this overstates what is assumed. The needed assumption is that no other factor caused the increase.
distractorRationale.E: Matching competitors' prices addresses competitiveness, not whether the card rather than some other change drove the rise in repeat visits.

explanation: The owner attributes the rise solely to the card; this holds only if no other factor (a new product, more foot traffic, etc.) explains the increase. (A) states exactly that assumption.

stem:
A town installed streetlights on several roads, and nighttime traffic accidents subsequently fell. Officials claim the lights reduced the accidents.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: The lights are costly to maintain.
A nearby factory closed during the same period, sharply reducing late-night driving on those roads.
Daytime accident counts were unchanged.
Drivers reported feeling safer after the lights were installed.
Neighboring towns also installed streetlights.

distractorRationale.A: Maintenance cost is irrelevant to whether the lights, rather than something else, reduced accidents; it attacks the policy's value, not the causal claim.
distractorRationale.C: Unchanged daytime counts are consistent with the lights working at night, so this fails to weaken the claim. The factory closure (B) supplies an alternative cause for the nighttime drop.
distractorRationale.D: Drivers feeling safer is subjective and, if anything, weakly supports the lights; it does not provide an alternative cause for fewer accidents.
distractorRationale.E: What neighboring towns did has no bearing on why accidents fell on these particular roads.

explanation: If far fewer cars were on the roads at night because the factory closed, the drop in accidents could be due to reduced exposure rather than the lights — undermining the causal claim.

stem:
A nutritionist argues that a new cereal lowers cholesterol, citing that participants who ate it daily for eight weeks had lower cholesterol afterward.

Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.text: The cereal tastes better than competing brands.
A comparable group who did not eat the cereal showed no change in cholesterol over the same eight weeks.
The cereal is high in dietary fiber.
Cholesterol can also be lowered through exercise.
The study ran for a full eight weeks.

distractorRationale.A: Better taste does not show the cereal lowers cholesterol; it is irrelevant to the causal claim. A control group with no change (B) isolates the cereal as the cause.
distractorRationale.C: High fiber merely offers a possible mechanism, but it does not establish that this cereal actually produced the observed drop in this study.
distractorRationale.D: That exercise can also lower cholesterol introduces an alternative cause, which weakens rather than strengthens the argument.
distractorRationale.E: Confirming the study ran the full eight weeks restates a premise and adds no support for the cause-and-effect conclusion.

explanation: A control group with no change rules out the possibility that cholesterol fell for everyone regardless of the cereal, isolating the cereal as the cause.

stem:
Only those who completed the safety course received a badge. No contractor completed the safety course.

Which of the following must be true?

choices.text: No contractor received a badge.
Every employee received a badge.
Some contractors received badges.
All badge holders are contractors.
The safety course was mandatory.

distractorRationale.B: Nothing is stated about employees or who did complete the course, so a universal claim about employees cannot be deduced. Only that no contractor got a badge follows.
distractorRationale.C: This directly contradicts the premises: since no contractor completed the course, none could have received a badge.
distractorRationale.D: The premises say badge holders completed the course, not that they are contractors; this reverses and overreaches the given relationship.
distractorRationale.E: Whether the course was mandatory is never addressed and cannot be inferred from the statements.

explanation: Since a badge requires completing the course and no contractor completed it, no contractor could have received a badge.

stem:
Critic: A poll shows that most readers favor the magazine's new layout, so the new layout is objectively better than the old one.

The critic's reasoning is most vulnerable to the objection that it

choices.text: fails to specify how much more readers preferred the new layout to the old one.
confuses what is popular with what is objectively better.
overlooks the possibility that readers who disliked the new layout did not respond to the poll.
assumes that a layout preferred today will still be preferred in the future.
treats the preferences of the magazine's readers as representative of all readers.

distractorRationale.A: The flaw is not a missing margin of preference; even a large majority would still only show popularity, not objective superiority.
distractorRationale.C: Possible non-response is a real survey concern but not the critic's central error, which is equating popularity with objective quality.
distractorRationale.D: The argument makes no claim about future preferences, so durability over time is not the vulnerability.
distractorRationale.E: Representativeness of all readers is a sampling issue, but the core flaw is treating what is popular as what is objectively better.

explanation: Preference among readers establishes popularity, not objective quality; treating the two as the same is the flaw.

stem:
Despite cutting its advertising budget in half this year, a company saw its sales rise.

Which of the following, if true, best explains this result?

choices.text: Each of the company's main competitors raised prices sharply this year, driving many of their customers to the company.
The company's past advertisements had won industry awards.
Advertising generally tends to increase a company's sales.
The company hired a new marketing director this year.
Sales fell at the company's largest rival the previous year.

distractorRationale.B: Past award-winning ads do not explain why sales rose this year after the budget was cut; it concerns prior advertising quality, not the current paradox.
distractorRationale.C: Saying advertising usually raises sales deepens the puzzle rather than resolving it, since the company spent less yet sold more.
distractorRationale.D: A new marketing director is too vague to explain the rise; it names no mechanism, whereas competitors raising prices (A) directly redirects customers.
distractorRationale.E: A rival's sales falling the previous year is about a different period and does not explain this year's increase.

explanation: A large shift of customers from competitors who raised prices explains how sales could rise even though the company spent less on advertising.

stem:
A city plans to reduce downtown congestion by raising parking fees.

To evaluate whether the plan will achieve its goal, it would be most useful to know which of the following?

choices.text: Whether most people who drive downtown have a practical alternative to driving there.
How much additional revenue the higher fees will raise.
When the new fees will take effect.
Which downtown streets are currently the busiest.
How neighboring cities set their parking fees.

distractorRationale.B: Revenue raised measures a side effect, not whether higher fees actually cut the number of cars downtown, which is the plan's stated goal.
distractorRationale.C: The timing of the fees does not help judge whether they will reduce congestion. Whether drivers have alternatives (A) is what determines success.
distractorRationale.D: Knowing which streets are busiest describes the current problem but does not test whether the fee increase will change driving behavior.
distractorRationale.E: Neighboring cities' fees are a comparison point, not evidence about how this city's drivers will respond.

explanation: If drivers have no alternative, higher fees will not reduce how many drive downtown; whether alternatives exist is decisive for the plan's success.

stem:
Manager: Our new sales-training program works — representatives who completed it now outperform those who did not.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion that the program causes better performance?

choices.text: The program is shorter than the previous one.
Only the company's most experienced representatives were selected to take the program.
Companywide sales rose this year.
The representatives said they enjoyed the program.
Training programs are common in the industry.

distractorRationale.A: A shorter program does not undermine the causal claim; it concerns the program's length, not whether prior skill explains the performance gap.
distractorRationale.C: A companywide sales rise affects everyone equally and so cannot explain why the trained group specifically outperforms the others.
distractorRationale.D: Representatives enjoying the program speaks to satisfaction, not to whether the program, rather than preexisting ability, caused better performance.
distractorRationale.E: That training is common in the industry is background and provides no alternative explanation for the gap. Selecting the most experienced reps (B) does.

explanation: If the strongest representatives were the ones chosen for the program, their superior performance may reflect prior skill, not the program — a selection bias that undercuts the causal claim.

stem:
Every book in the store's sale section is a paperback. Some paperbacks in the store are not on sale.

Which of the following must be true?

choices.text: Some books in the store are not in the sale section.
All paperbacks in the store are on sale.
No hardcover books are in the store.
Some books in the sale section are hardcovers.
Most of the store's books are paperbacks.

distractorRationale.B: This contradicts the premise that some paperbacks are not on sale, so it cannot follow.
distractorRationale.C: Nothing rules out hardcovers existing in the store; the premises only describe the sale section and paperbacks.
distractorRationale.D: Every sale-section book is a paperback, so claiming some are hardcovers directly contradicts the premise.
distractorRationale.E: No information is given about the proportion of paperbacks, so 'most' cannot be inferred. Only that some books lie outside the sale section follows.

explanation: A paperback that is not on sale cannot be in the sale section, so at least one book in the store lies outside the sale section.

stem:
Researcher: Cities with more bike lanes tend to have lower obesity rates, so building bike lanes reduces obesity.

Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.text: Bike lanes are popular with city residents.
In several cities, obesity rates declined in the years after new bike lanes were added.
People who are obese rarely ride bicycles.
Bike lanes reduce automobile traffic.
Cycling burns a substantial number of calories.

distractorRationale.A: Popularity of bike lanes does not show they reduce obesity; it is irrelevant to the causal claim about the correlation.
distractorRationale.C: That obese people rarely cycle suggests reverse causation or self-selection, which weakens rather than strengthens the argument.
distractorRationale.D: Reduced car traffic is a different effect of bike lanes and does not connect them to lower obesity. Obesity falling after lanes are added (B) supports the causal direction.
distractorRationale.E: Cycling burning calories is general background; it does not establish that adding bike lanes actually lowered obesity in these cities.

explanation: Showing that obesity fell after bike lanes were added supports the direction of causation, rather than merely a correlation between the two.

stem:
Politician: My opponent argues that we should cut public spending, but he once overspent his own campaign budget. His proposal should therefore be rejected.

The politician's argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

choices.text: attacks the opponent's character instead of the merits of his proposal.
assumes that a single instance of overspending reveals a consistent pattern.
fails to specify how much public spending should be cut.
overlooks the possibility that cutting public spending could harm the economy.
takes for granted that managing a campaign budget resembles managing public funds.

distractorRationale.B: Whether one instance shows a pattern is a secondary concern; even a genuine pattern of overspending would still not address the proposal's merits, which is the primary flaw.
distractorRationale.C: Failing to specify the amount of cuts is not the error; the argument's problem is attacking the person, not the lack of detail in the proposal.
distractorRationale.D: Possible economic harm from cuts is a substantive counterpoint the politician never raises, but the criticized flaw is the ad hominem attack itself.
distractorRationale.E: The weak analogy between campaign and public funds is a real gap, yet the more fundamental flaw is rejecting the proposal by attacking the opponent's character (A).

explanation: Citing the opponent's past behavior rather than addressing the proposal itself is an ad hominem attack, which does not bear on whether the proposal is sound.

stem:
A store moved its bestselling items from the front to the back. Those items then sold slightly less, yet the store's overall sales increased.

Which of the following best explains the increase in overall sales?

choices.text: The bestselling items continued to draw customers into the store after the move.
To reach the bestsellers at the back, customers walked past many other products and bought more of them.
The bestselling items still accounted for most of the store's individual sales.
Customers who came for the bestsellers spent slightly less on those items than before.
Moving the bestsellers freed up space near the entrance for a new display.

distractorRationale.A: If the bestsellers still drew customers in, that explains foot traffic but not the increase in overall sales; the lift comes from those customers buying other items (B).
distractorRationale.C: That bestsellers still account for most individual sales does not explain how total sales rose while those very items sold less.
distractorRationale.D: Customers spending slightly less on the bestsellers describes the decline, deepening the puzzle rather than explaining the overall increase.
distractorRationale.E: Freeing up entrance space is vague and names no actual added sales, whereas routing customers past more goods (B) directly drives the increase.

explanation: Routing customers past more merchandise raises purchases of other items, which can lift overall sales even as the bestsellers sell a bit less.

stem:
A school district will provide every student with a tablet in order to raise test scores, reasoning that students who already own tablets tend to score higher.

The plan depends on which of the following assumptions?

choices.text: Tablets are affordable for the district to purchase in bulk.
The higher scores of current tablet owners are due to the tablets themselves, not to other advantages those students have.
Every student will actually use the tablet provided.
Teachers in the district support the change.
The tablets will remain usable for several years.

distractorRationale.A: Affordability concerns feasibility, not whether tablets cause higher scores; the plan can be assumed affordable yet still fail if tablets are not the true cause.
distractorRationale.C: Whether students use the tablets is a separate practical condition; the core assumption is that the tablet itself, not other advantages, produces the higher scores.
distractorRationale.D: Teacher support relates to implementation, not to whether the correlation between tablet ownership and scores reflects causation.
distractorRationale.E: How long the tablets last addresses durability, not the causal assumption that tablets, rather than students' other advantages, raise scores.

explanation: The plan assumes the tablet causes the higher scores. If tablet owners score higher because of other advantages (e.g., wealth), giving tablets to all students would not reproduce the effect.

stem:
An airline claims that its new boarding procedure speeds up boarding compared with the old one.

To evaluate this claim, it would be most helpful to determine which of the following?

choices.text: Whether the new procedure was tested on flights of the same size and aircraft type as the old procedure.
Whether the time saved during boarding allows the airline's flights to depart on schedule.
Whether the airlines that board fastest all use a procedure like the new one.
Whether the new procedure speeds up the unloading of passengers as well.
Whether gate agents followed the new procedure correctly on every test flight.

distractorRationale.B: On-time departures measure a downstream benefit, not whether the new procedure itself is faster than the old; a fair head-to-head comparison (A) is what is needed.
distractorRationale.C: What the fastest-boarding airlines use is an outside correlation that does not test this airline's own before-and-after claim.
distractorRationale.D: Speeding up unloading concerns a different process entirely and is irrelevant to evaluating the boarding-speed claim.
distractorRationale.E: Whether agents followed the procedure correctly matters only secondarily; the decisive issue is whether the two procedures were tested under comparable conditions.

explanation: A fair comparison requires similar conditions; if the procedures were tested on very different flights, any difference in boarding time may be due to those differences rather than the procedure.

stem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

choices.text: argue that coral gardening should be abandoned in favor of breeding heat-tolerant strains.
describe efforts to restore coral reefs while noting their limitations and the need to address ocean warming.
prove that coral reefs can no longer be saved.
compare the financial costs of two restoration techniques in detail.
celebrate the success of underwater coral nurseries.

distractorRationale.A: The passage does not advocate abandoning coral gardening; it presents both gardening and heat-tolerant strains as complementary efforts, so this misstates the purpose.
distractorRationale.C: The passage is not defeatist about reefs; it describes restoration efforts and their value alongside emissions cuts, the opposite of declaring reefs unsavable.
distractorRationale.D: A detailed cost comparison of two techniques is not the focus; the passage gives a balanced overview rather than a financial analysis.
distractorRationale.E: The passage notes limitations rather than simply celebrating nurseries, so an unqualified celebration overstates its tone.

explanation: The passage presents restoration techniques, their drawbacks, and the conclusion that they help only alongside emissions cuts — a balanced overview, which (B) captures.

stem: According to the passage, coral gardening involves

choices.text: breeding heat-tolerant strains in laboratories.
growing coral fragments in nurseries and transplanting them onto reefs.
reducing greenhouse-gas emissions at their source.
relocating fish to degraded reefs.
covering the ocean floor with artificial structures.

distractorRationale.A: Breeding heat-tolerant strains in labs describes the 'super corals' approach, not coral gardening, which grows and transplants fragments.
distractorRationale.C: Cutting emissions at the source is the broader solution the passage urges, not the definition of coral gardening.
distractorRationale.D: Relocating fish is never described as part of coral gardening, which involves growing coral fragments and transplanting them onto reefs.
distractorRationale.E: Covering the ocean floor with artificial structures is not what the passage describes; gardening means transplanting nursery-grown coral fragments.

explanation: The passage defines coral gardening as growing fragments in underwater nurseries and transplanting them onto degraded reefs.

stem: The passage suggests that "super corals" would be especially valuable because they

choices.text: grow faster than gardened corals.
could survive the heat stress that threatens ordinary corals.
are cheaper to produce than nursery corals.
would increase the genetic diversity of reefs.
eliminate the need to reduce emissions.

distractorRationale.A: The passage values super corals for surviving heat, not for faster growth; growth speed is not the stated benefit.
distractorRationale.C: Lower cost is not the cited advantage; super corals are valued because they can withstand the heat stress that threatens ordinary corals.
distractorRationale.D: Genetic diversity is not the reason given; their value lies in tolerating future warming.
distractorRationale.E: The passage stresses that emissions still must be reduced, so super corals do not eliminate that need.

explanation: Super corals are described as naturally heat-tolerant strains that "might withstand future warming," so their value lies in surviving heat stress.

stem: The author's attitude toward coral restoration is best described as

choices.text: unreserved enthusiasm.
open hostility.
cautious support.
complete indifference.
resigned hopelessness.

distractorRationale.A: Unreserved enthusiasm overstates the author, who repeatedly notes limitations and conditions rather than praising without reservation.
distractorRationale.B: There is no hostility; the author acknowledges encouraging results, indicating support rather than opposition.
distractorRationale.D: The author is clearly engaged with the topic, so indifference contradicts the evident concern and qualified endorsement.
distractorRationale.E: The author is not hopeless; noting that restoration helps alongside emissions cuts reflects cautious support, not resignation.

explanation: The author acknowledges encouraging results yet repeatedly notes limitations and conditions, reflecting cautious support.

stem: The passage is primarily concerned with

choices.text: predicting the imminent collapse of major cities.
examining how remote work is reshaping, rather than emptying, urban areas.
advising individual workers on where they should live.
criticizing the tax policies of municipal governments.
describing the software tools that make remote work possible.

distractorRationale.A: The passage explicitly argues cities are being reshaped, not collapsing, so predicting imminent collapse contradicts its thesis.
distractorRationale.C: The passage analyzes a broad trend rather than advising individual workers on where to live.
distractorRationale.D: Municipal tax policies are mentioned only as strained budgets, not criticized; this is a detail, not the primary concern.
distractorRationale.E: The software enabling remote work is not the focus; the passage concerns how remote work redistributes urban activity.

explanation: The passage argues that work is being redistributed within and around cities rather than abandoned, making (B) the central concern.

stem: According to the passage, which of the following has proved resilient despite the shift to remote work?

choices.text: Downtown office occupancy.
Commercial property tax revenue.
Residential demand in many large cities.
Daily commuting into city centers.
Downtown foot traffic.

distractorRationale.A: Downtown office occupancy is presented as having declined, not as resilient; the passage names residential demand as resilient.
distractorRationale.B: Commercial property tax revenue is described as strained, the opposite of resilient.
distractorRationale.D: Daily commuting into city centers has fallen with remote work, so it is not the resilient element.
distractorRationale.E: Downtown foot traffic is cited among the strained items, whereas residential demand in large cities is what proved resilient.

explanation: The passage states that "residential demand in many large cities has proved resilient."

stem: The passage implies that some municipal budgets are strained because they

choices.text: depend heavily on downtown commercial activity and property taxes.
are legally unable to tax remote workers.
have stopped receiving any transit funding.
rely chiefly on tourism for revenue.
are controlled by suburban governments.

distractorRationale.B: The passage attributes strained budgets to dependence on downtown activity, not to any legal inability to tax remote workers.
distractorRationale.C: Nothing says transit funding stopped entirely; the strain stems from reliance on commercial property taxes and downtown foot traffic.
distractorRationale.D: Tourism is not named as the chief revenue source; the passage points to commercial property taxes and downtown activity.
distractorRationale.E: The passage does not say suburban governments control these budgets; the strain comes from dependence on downtown commerce.

explanation: The passage says the redistribution strains budgets "that depend on commercial property taxes and downtown foot traffic," implying reliance on downtown activity.

stem: The author mentions that former commuters now spend their "lunch hours and salaries" near neighborhood centers in order to

choices.text: criticize remote workers for spending money carelessly.
illustrate how economic activity is being redistributed geographically.
argue that suburbs are superior places to live than downtowns.
explain why firms have reduced their office space.
show that daily commuting has increased.

distractorRationale.A: The detail is not a criticism of careless spending; it illustrates where economic activity is now occurring.
distractorRationale.C: The author makes no claim that suburbs are superior places to live; the example shows redistribution of activity, not a ranking.
distractorRationale.D: Reduced office space is a separate point; the lunch-hours detail illustrates the geographic shift of spending, not why firms cut space.
distractorRationale.E: The example shows spending moving to neighborhood centers, not that commuting has increased; commuting has in fact declined.

explanation: The detail is offered as a concrete example of activity shifting away from downtown cores toward outlying areas.

stem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

choices.text: trace the rise of the free public library and the debates that surrounded it.
argue that subscription libraries were superior to free ones.
describe the architecture of nineteenth-century library buildings.
prove that public libraries measurably reduced crime.
criticize philanthropists for the conditions they attached to donations.

distractorRationale.B: The passage does not argue subscription libraries were superior; it traces the rise of free libraries and the debate around them.
distractorRationale.C: Library architecture is not discussed; the focus is the emergence of the free public library and its surrounding debates.
distractorRationale.D: The passage mentions crime reduction only as an argument supporters made, not as a proven measurable result.
distractorRationale.E: Philanthropists' conditions are a detail, not the central purpose; the passage's aim is to trace the free library's rise and the debates over it.

explanation: The passage narrates the emergence of the free public library and the arguments for and against it, which (A) summarizes.

stem: According to the passage, subscription libraries differed from free public libraries in that they

choices.text: were funded entirely by philanthropists.
required users to pay fees for access.
contained only practical, non-fiction books.
were open exclusively to workers.
were maintained by municipal governments.

distractorRationale.A: The passage attributes paid-fee access to subscription libraries; being funded entirely by philanthropists is not the stated distinction.
distractorRationale.C: Nothing restricts subscription libraries to non-fiction; the difference the passage names is that they charged annual fees.
distractorRationale.D: Subscription libraries were open to those who could pay, not exclusively to workers.
distractorRationale.E: Municipal maintenance describes free public libraries, not the subscription ones, which required paid fees.

explanation: The passage says earlier collections were "subscription-based, open only to those who could pay annual fees."

stem: Which of the following is identified in the passage as an argument made by supporters of free libraries?

choices.text: Unrestricted access to novels would corrupt public morals.
Taxpayers should not be made to fund strangers' reading.
Libraries would reduce crime by offering alternatives to idleness.
Access to books should depend on a person's wealth.
Philanthropists should retain control over the collections.

distractorRationale.A: Fear that novels would corrupt morals is presented as an objection to free libraries, not an argument from supporters.
distractorRationale.B: The objection that taxpayers should not fund strangers' reading is an opponents' argument, the opposite of what supporters claimed.
distractorRationale.D: Tying book access to wealth reflects the subscription model supporters opposed, not an argument they made.
distractorRationale.E: Philanthropists retaining control is not cited as a supporters' argument; supporters argued libraries would reduce crime by countering idleness.

explanation: Supporters "countered that libraries would reduce crime by offering productive alternatives to idleness," matching (C). The other options are objections or unrelated.

stem: The passage suggests that tying libraries to local tax revenue resulted in

choices.text: uniformly excellent collections across the country.
the end of all philanthropic donations.
unequal library quality between richer and poorer communities.
a return to subscription fees.
a reduction in overall public access to books.

distractorRationale.A: The passage says collections varied with local wealth, so uniformly excellent collections contradicts its point.
distractorRationale.B: It does not claim philanthropic donations ended; the noted result was uneven quality between richer and poorer communities.
distractorRationale.D: Tax funding moved away from subscription fees, so a return to fees is not what the passage describes.
distractorRationale.E: Overall access expanded with free libraries; the passage notes unequal quality, not a reduction in access.

explanation: The passage states the arrangement left "poorer communities with thinner collections," implying quality varied with local wealth.
